summary:
When performing occlusal equilibration, it is important to consider both upper and lower buccal areas as they play complementary roles in maintaining stable occlusion and preventing imbalances. Bilateral approach ensures comprehensive management of various issues while adapting to natural variations between individuals' dental structures. To maintain proper bite alignment and support functional aesthetics effectively, equal attention should be paid to adjusting these regions together rather than focusing solely on one over another based strictly upon isolated anatomical features without regard for systemic interaction effects within oral biomechanics. Agent1 suggests a balanced focus on both upper and lower buccal reductions during occlusal equilibration due to mutual dependencies and variability in patient anatomy; an inclusive method better addresses diverse challenges by promoting even force distribution and enhancing long-term functionality and esthetics compared to unilateral adjustments alone.
